intro:
Nakedness in Scripture is rarely merely physical; it is an image of exposure — of being uncovered, defenseless, and seen as one truly is. Closely bound to it is shame, the painful awareness of guilt and disgrace before God and others. Together they trace one of the Bible's deepest wounds: the loss of innocent openness, and the longing to be covered, accepted, and unashamed again.

The motif is set at creation. The man and his wife "were both naked and were not ashamed" (Genesis 2:25) — a picture of unbroken trust, nothing hidden between them or before God. Sin shatters it at once: their eyes are opened, they know they are naked, they sew fig leaves and hide from the LORD, confessing, "I was afraid, because I was naked, and I hid myself" (Genesis 3:7; Genesis 3:10). Their own coverings prove inadequate; it is God who graciously clothes them with garments of skin, the first hint that the shame of sin must be covered by a death not their own (Genesis 3:21).

From there nakedness becomes a recurring sign of vulnerability and judgment. The exposure of Noah's nakedness brings disgrace on his house (Genesis 9:21–23); the priests must wear linen "to cover their naked flesh" before the Holy One (Exodus 28:42); and the prophets portray unfaithful Israel and proud Babylon as a harlot whose nakedness is uncovered in shame as the penalty for sin (Ezekiel 16:37; Isaiah 47:3). To be stripped is to be judged; to be clothed is to be honored and restored.

The gospel answers the wound at its root. On the cross Jesus is stripped and exposed, "despising the shame" (Hebrews 12:2), bearing in his own body the disgrace that sin deserves. In exchange he clothes his people: the risen Christ counsels the church to buy from him "white garments so that you may clothe yourself and the shame of your nakedness may not be seen" (Revelation 3:18), and the redeemed are wrapped in robes of righteousness and salvation (Isaiah 61:10). So the promise stands: "Whoever believes in him will not be put to shame" (Romans 10:11).

The motif thus runs from Eden's lost innocence to the cross that bears our shame, ending where it began — a people unashamed in God's presence, no longer hiding, fully covered by grace.
